Trom Cork we received a note on Monday, com-
raencing this way: ““ Dear Mr, Editor—Irom Listowel
to Wesford? Will you give this intolerant tolerance-
provers a literary hiding?’  Our correspondent also
“Incidents of this kind only show that much as
:ADER has done to make Catholic Trishmen seli-
respecting and hold up their heads, it is only'scmtc}!:
ing the surface of the thick hides of many tame slaves.

“In the course of a letter from a town in Connacht,

received by the same post, the writer remarks: Gl
hope you will score the Wesford County Council for
their resolution about the holding of ‘the sehdlarships in

Trinity College.” Indeed, we were just sitting down
with pen in hand to put down what we thought of the
majority at this meeting of the Wexford County Col}ﬂ-
cil when we received the following from yet a third
correspondent:— “ The decision of the Wexford County

Council Jlast week, to include Trimby in its
scholarship scheme, must indeed have caused
a gasp of surprise among Ivish Irelanders

reason is assigned for such an extraordinary decision,
unless indeed a eall for ‘toleration’ can be called a
reason. Toleration to whom? One cannot, I take if,
be ‘tolerant’ to the top dog, and in endowment,
wealth, social prestige, and political power, Trinity is
still the top dog, and the Wexford County Council have
forgotten that. ‘Tolerance’ to an alien within the
gates, to a foreigner that could be a citizen, but who
has deliberately spurned the offer of citizenship, is
cowardice and to ourselves injustice.  ‘ Tolerance,”

financially to a richer rival, is to be generous
before just; chari begins at home. ‘ Toler-
ance’ to a chartered body that has done its
bad best to kill Irish, from a county whose

in the Gaelic Revival is known everywhere,
is an Irish anomaly.. ‘Tolerance’ to a body that
through its educational representatives has nearly
ruined Ireland, and that at the present raoment sup-
plies the educational virus by which the National Board
striles down teachers and language equally, is a weak-
minded policy even unto madness. The most pro-
minent meml of the National Board are Doctor
Starkie and Provost Traill. ‘Tolerance ’ to the self-
declared enemy, to the corporation that proudly declares
itself Protestant and English, to the pampered body
for which not even its own Unionist {riendly Councils
have struck one farthing, surely that, on the part of
the Wexford County Council, is folly.""

great wor

Our correspondent goes on:—‘ And at whose ex-
pense? Our own University, that one that will repre-
sent Wexford, will suffer for every scholar sent else-
where. The University that is for the Irish people,
thatt has hound itself to answer to the people’s call for
nationality, that has decided to live with and for the
people, will suffer through every people’s scholar who
goes elsewhere.  The people’s University will be

* penalised in fayour of the traditional enemy of the Irish

people, that has been false to our ideals, and false to
our counfry, and that still to-day counters the people
in their evc ffort to secure full nationalify, and still
proudly declares itself English and Protestant. What
has come over Wexford? The  tolerant * action of the
Wexford County Council is ‘ intolerant ’ to the National
University. That University is still comparatively
poor and needs endowment and scholars. Its proud
aim and ideal is to be the Irish University in Ireland;
it has made Trish essential in all its students, and it
justly demands in return the full support of all al
hodi Such action as that of Wexford, in leaving the
way frée to deprive her of scholars and funds, and to
hand -over to the alien institution that has chosen the
broad and easy path of ‘ No Irish," will tend to impede
the great nafional work of regeneration which lies
vefore her. - One can only hope tliat in this matter the
Wexford County Council does not represent Wexford."*

We have little or nothing to add after that. Per-
haps our correspondent is a little too enthusiastic over
the: National ; but even if he is that in no way lessens
his case againat the Wexford County Council.  The
“ National " was watered down so as to be acceptable

T P
s
G20

to Turk, Jew, Heathen . and Protestant as weli as
Catholic, and its Senate was well watered too—by the
English Government. The very fact that the Univer-
sity was made religiously nondescript by the English
Government in order, apparently, to appease the non-
Catholics, and that the authorities of the University so
far as they were selected by the English Government
‘were carefully graded is all the more reason why the
eountry should ery shame at the action of the Wextord
County Council.

The ‘‘ National ” University is a watered-down thing,
but it is for us to make it as National as we can. The
proceedings, as reported ab the recent Conferring, shows

_how -far removed the Univers is in fact from being
National. There was nothing National and certainly
nothing original nor humourous in tlinging toy bomb-
shells on the occasion. If the undergraduates were
more National and less bratty, we might expect that
they would have sung a few songs, or chorn
Irish; that there would be some National in
at the bottom of any row they might like to kick
and some National flavour about theiv horse-play.
Montenegrins are facing real bombshells in 1
but the poor de-nationalised imitators at the *“National
Conferring threw toy bombshells. We think the lads
ought to be heartily ashamed of themselves, particularly
{or the utter lack of originality and Nationality in their
display. Theve is, perhaps, something to be said in
tenuation of their conduct. After all what can one
expect from a ‘‘ Nati " University with Christy
Nixon as its Vice-Chancellor? What is there Trish or
National about that person? By the way, his picture
ac Vice-Chancellor was published in a paper recently,
and until we looked at the name under the picture we
shought it was supposed to represent a “ Beefeater
i a Gilbert and Sullivan oper: A ““ National ’’ Uni-
versity that contains such Senaters as Windle, the
Lord Chief Baron, Judge Barton, Charles O'Connor,
Sir George Roche, ‘Sir Stanley Harrington, Butcher,
M.P., is somewhat comical, and perhaps, it is not to
be so much wondered at that the hobbledchoys on
Lonferring Day made asses of themselves.

The Bantry All-for-Treland Brass Band was imported
from England. A local paper tells its readers:—* The
many subseribers to the funds of the above Band, and

. All-for-Trelanders in general, will be pleased to learn
that a splendid set of instruments for a full
brass and reed band has been purchased from
Messrs. —, of Tondon. The Band will
be known -as the Bantry All-for-Treland- Brass
Band, and will be an additional tower of strength to
the All-for-Treland League which is already impregnable
in the historic town of Banfry and district, the birth-
place of Treland’s National Poet and the other dis-
tinguished members of the famous ‘ Bantry Band.” "
We are further informed that these English-made in-
struments were on show on the premises of Mr. James
Gilhooly, M.P., for a few days; it is a wonder that
James did not give a tune on one of the instruments

Bandon, in the County of Cork, has a ““ Star *’ Banjo
and Mandoline Orchestral Society.  There are four
main classes in Bandon. There are the “avistocrats,””
the middle class, the snoboeracy and the democracy.
The snobocracy are the class from which the ** Star™”
Banjo and Mandoline Orch Society are mainly re-
cruited, junior clerks in banks, Pawns and their assis-
tants, some Post Office officials and the-like. A man
by the name of Birrell,” from Cork—nothing to the
amusing and eloquent Mr. Birrell—a professor of music,
honoured Bandon some years ago by settling there. He
opened a banjo, etc., shop there, and we understand
that if you bought a banjo you were freely taught to
play it—certainly a most enterprising gentleman and
a credit to the name. Tn due course the Star Banjo
and Mandoline Society held a concert under the baton
of the namesalke of the illustrious funny man of West-
minster.  After that there was a tour through the dis-
triet, and all concerts were followed by suppers and
dances. Tiast year this philantrophic crowd advertised *
a concerb in aid of the local Coal Fund, and it was a
greab suceess, though we understand that one eftect of
it was that ordinary public subseriptions to the Coal
Tund fell a bit short.  After the concert there was an

On.S.u'nday, November 24th, Rev. Vincent Byrne,
S.J., will preach the annual Charity Sermon in aid of
St. Brigid’s Orphanage, 46 Eccles Streot, Dublin. The
sermon will be preached after the first Gospel of last
Mass_ at the Church of Lrancis Xavier, Upper
Gardiner Street. St. Brigid’s Orphanage, which is in
charge of the Sisters of the Holy Faith, was founded
nearly sixty years ago for the purpose of saving Catholic
. orphans and destitute children in danger of loss of
Faith. Those who cannot conveniently be present on
the oceasion of the Charity Sermon may send their con-
tributions to this desorving charity to His Grace the
Archbishop, to the preacher, Father Byme, S.J., to
any of the clergy in the city, or direct to St. Brigid’s
House, 46 Eccles Street, Dublin.

sions without personally attending. Picture a lot of
grown men—Tories—hidden away in all sorts of places
—the ‘‘ snuggeries” of Bung we suppose are a favour-
ite haunt—and some grown boy scouts called whips
peeping and prying into the House of Commons and
into the comings and goings of members, and at a
- given moment mustering all the old boys in hiding and
rushing them into the House with a view to making a
temporary and bogus majority. It is really very silly,
yet the game appears to be one of the great, and one
| of the most serious games, in the British House of
i Commons., The game was successful on Monday and
ol the ha’penny papers had a fine time with their stop
| press nuisance, and hundreds of leading articles and
columns of surmises, comments and guesses flood the
papers on Tuesday.

It is all very silly, but some of the politicians believe,
or make-believe, it to be very important. It is cer-
tainly important in so far as it upsets arrangements
somewhat and retards the progress of the Home Rule
Bill. As the English play their politics in this hide
and seek sort of way it is for the Irish to face the fact
and be in full strength whilst the men scouts of the
Unionists are looking for a chance to rush into the
nursery, or Parliament, and outnumber the Home
Rulers amidst cheers such as only children in earnest
at their game can give.

The ixhibition which will be opened next Monday
at the Rotunda Rink, ‘‘ Commerce,”” will be unique
in many respects. It is the first time a Business
Exhibition on a large scale has ever been attempted
in Ireland. We are assured there will be a plentiful
array of exhibits of interest to the business man. These
will include up-to-date filing systems, adding machines;
electric typewriters, and in fact all the newest methods
in office and business equipment. There will be de-
monstrations of various appliances each day, and a
novelty in the form of a salesmanship competition, in
which the competitors will be required to demonstrate
their ability to sell goods which are not asked for. Othey
competitions have been arranged in business letter-
writing, shorthand and typewriting, and have, we un-
derstand, attracted a very share of attention.
Tach evening a lecture on 10 important business
matter will be delivered in the Pillar Room or Tar
Concert Hall, Rotunda, and will be followed by a dis-
cussion in which many of the leading business men
will take part. Altogether ** Conimerce * promises to
be a big success. Certiinly it should be visited by

every business man who values the economy of offi-

I T is only

as the result of placing in juxta-position the

respective classes of Dublin and Belfast, into

which the Census Commissioners at successive enu-

merations have divided the population, that it is pos-

sible to realise the impudence of the contention that

any increase in the cost of postage, or in extra taxation,
must fall mainly on Belfast.

The Dublin of the nsus Commissioners has an
area of 7,911 acres; that of Belfast being 15,139 acres,
or nearly double as much. If the area of Dublin were
extended to about the same extent as that of Belfast
it would not only include Pembroke, Rathmines, Rath-
gar, and Dublin’s villadom generally, but Kingstown,
Terenure, and Blackrock. The exclusion of these com-
paratively wealthy portions of the city's overflow f'le[l“)'
handicap Ireland’s capital when making comparisons;
whether we take Income Tax asse smcnts or educa-
tional or vital or®health statistics, or llousing accom-
modation, or populational increases. Thus the Com-
missioners, on page 11 of their Preliminary Report,
return Belfast as having increased its population dur-
ing the decennium by 10.4 per cent., Dublin having
only done so by 6.4 per cent. From the same table,
however, we learn that Rathmines and Rathgar in-
creased by 17.1 per cent., and Pembroke by 13.4, these
increases being notoriously effected at the expense of the
older and less advancing portions of the city's north side,
accelerated by rail and tram facilities. Before resum-
ing our examination of the Census Summaries, the
Belfast speech, delivered on the 5th inst., by the Pro-
testant Bishop of Down and Connor, at the annual
meefing of his Diocesan Synod, calls for more than
passing notice. The speech occupies two columns and
a-half of the ““Daily Express,’”’ and simply bristles with
statistics; a circumstance which may or may not have
resulted from the presence on the platform of Mr.
Arthur Samuels, K.C., and his appointment as the
Bishop's assessor.

to show from the Census Summaries how immensely—if
not in every respect, at all events in every respect dealt

with the whole of Ireland in some cases, and Ulster in

must know this, if the Bishop does not.

and concerning which much bad blood was aroused.
Then, in the seventh century the Emperor Heraclius
confirmed the Servians in the possession of this terri-
tory on condition that they should protect it and the
Empire against the attacks of the Avars and Huns.
They remained guardians of the empire. At the end
of the ninth century the Servians became Christians,
and by the termination of the tenth century they were
in possession of the whole country lying between the
Adriatic and the Danube—then divided into two parts,
Adriatic Servia comprising Zeta,-Tavania, Hum and
Neretva, and Danubian Servia comprising Bosnia and
Rascia. The Servian tribes were hemmed in between
the Frank and Bulgavian Empires, and they consequently
often suffered from these Imperial rivalvies, but
they preserved their independence and resisted success-
iully both the attacks of Byzantium and the Bulgars.

the help of the Byzantine Empire, in uniting Danubian
and Adriatic Servia, but after his death the Greeks
took possession of cia, and all the national spirit
seemed to have thereby passed out of Adriatic Servia.

1381, reigned in Servia. He married Helen, the Bul-
cavian Tsar's daughter, and, by the way it may be
mentioned, that it was from the Bulgars this title of
Tsar or Czar is derived. In the year 1344 Dushan con-
vened an assembly at Uskub, the old capital of Servia
(won back by the Servians last week, now occupied
amidst the joy of the inhabitants by them, and never
to be swrendered). Here he promulgated the cele-
brated €ode of laws known as the Zakonik, a code em-
